title: Low Back Pain — Mechanical
oneLiner: Benign musculoskeletal pain from lumbar strain or degeneration; affects up to 80% of adults, usually self-limiting, but mimics can kill or paralyze.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 35-year-old manual worker presents to the emergency department after lifting a heavy box at work. He felt a sudden “pop” in his lower back followed by sharp, localised pain. He can only walk if hunched forward, and bending makes it worse. Lying flat with a pillow under his knees eases the pain. He has no leg pain, no numbness, no bladder or bowel disturbance. Vital signs are normal. Straight leg raise is slightly limited by back pain (not by radicular pain) and there is no weakness, reflex loss, or saddle anaesthesia.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Sudden onset with lifting | Acute myofascial strain or disc stress; no time for insidious pathology |
| Pain localised to lower back (no leg pain) | No nerve root irritation; likely muscle, ligament, or facet joint |
| Pain worse with bending, better lying flat | Mechanical nature – movement aggravates, rest relieves (vs inflammatory pain where rest worsens) |
| No leg pain, no neurological signs | Radiculopathy unlikely; cauda equina syndrome ruled out |
| Normal vital signs, no fever | Infection, inflammatory disease, and malignancy less likely |
| Age 35 | Degenerative change unlikely (though possible); discogenic or muscular cause |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Older patient (65+)** with vague, dull low back ache – may be degenerative facet arthritis or undiagnosed compression fracture; “mechanical” label can mask osteoporosis or metastasis.
- **Young athlete** with unilateral lower back pain worse on hyperextension – think spondylolysis (stress fracture of pars interarticularis) rather than simple strain.
- **Postpartum woman** with deep, aching sacral pain – may be sacroiliac joint dysfunction, but also consider occult osteomyelitis pubis or sacral stress fracture.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
Mechanical low back pain is pain originating from the musculoskeletal structures of the lumbar spine (muscles, ligaments, intervertebral discs, facet joints) in the absence of systemic disease, infection, cancer, or nerve compression. It is a diagnosis of exclusion that relies on a benign history and examination.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** ~80% of adults experience at least one episode. It’s the leading cause of years lived with disability globally.
- **Stakes:** Most resolves in 4–6 weeks with simple analgesia and active management. But missing a red flag (cauda equina, infection, fracture, malignancy) can lead to permanent paralysis, sepsis, or death.
- **Common pitfall:** Over-investigation (MRI in the first 4–6 weeks) leads to incidental findings (disc bulges, mild degeneration) that don’t correlate with symptoms, causing unnecessary anxiety and surgery. Conversely, under-investigation of red flags is catastrophic.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how it develops:**
1. **Acute strain:** A sudden force or awkward twist exceeds the microtensile strength of lumbar muscles and ligaments. Small tears → local haemorrhage → inflammatory cascade (prostaglandins, bradykinin) → muscle spasm as a protective reflex. The spasm itself is painful and stiffens the spine.
2. **Degenerative disc / facet joint:** Over years, the annulus fibrosus weakens, allowing the nucleus pulposus to bulge (but not herniate into the canal). Inflammatory mediators from annular tears irritate the sinuvertebral nerve, generating localised pain without root compression. Facet joint osteoarthritis causes chondrocyte injury, joint effusion, and capsular stretch → referred pain to the buttock or posterior thigh.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Pain with flexion (bending forward):** Stretches already inflamed posterior ligaments and increases intradiscal pressure; also compresses the anterior annulus.
- **Pain relief lying flat:** Unloads the spine – disc pressure drops from ~100 kg standing to ~25 kg supine.
- **Hunched (antalgic) gait:** Reflex paravertebral muscle spasm splints the spine in a position that maximises canal diameter (slight flexion) and minimises tension on injured structures.
- **Absence of leg pain / neurologic signs:** Because the pathology is confined to the outer disc annulus, muscles, or facet joints – no nerve root compression or cauda equina irritation.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**
The “mechanical” nature (worse with activity, better with rest) differentiates it from **inflammatory** back pain (ankylosing spondylitis) where rest makes it worse and morning stiffness lasts >30 minutes, and from **tumour/infection** pain which is constant, progressive, and often nocturnal.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | 1-line “stem clue” that points to it |
|----------|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Non-specific back strain | No specific trauma, no red flags, well young adult |
| **Mechanical – acute** | Lumbar muscle/ligament sprain | “Lifting heavy object”, sudden onset, localised tenderness |
| **Mechanical – discogenic** | Degenerative disc disease without herniation | Chronic ache with flares; worse sitting long periods; no leg pain |
| **Mechanical – articular** | Facet joint osteoarthritis | Pain with extension (arching back) and rotation; tenderness paraspinal |
| **Mechanical – structural** | Spondylolysis / spondylolisthesis | Young athlete, pain on hyperextension, may have mild radicular symptoms |
| **Sacroiliac joint dysfunction** | Ligamentous strain of SIJ | Unilateral buttock pain, worse with single-leg stance, positive Fortin finger test |
| **Ligamentous** | Interspinous ligament sprain (e.g., after hyperflexion) | Pain directly over spinous processes; reproduces with springing test |

**Highlight most common cause:** Non-specific mechanical LBP (~90% of all acute episodes). Specific diagnosis often elusive – it’s the pattern that matters, not the label.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
- **STarT Back Screening Tool** (for risk stratification in primary care): 9 questions plus disability scale. Categorises patients into low/medium/high risk. Low risk → reassure, self-manage. Medium/high risk → consider physiotherapy or psychosocial intervention.  
- **No validated diagnostic criteria for mechanical LBP itself** – it’s a clinical diagnosis.

**For each test:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **None (initial)** | – | Red flags absent; most LBP needs no imaging | A recent trial (Lancet, 2023) showed early MRI in simple LBP increased surgery rate without better outcomes |
| **X-ray (2 views)** | Alignment, bone density, disc height, fractures, spondylolisthesis | Suspected fracture (fall/osteoporosis), suspected spondylolisthesis, age >50 with first episode | Loss of height + wedge deformity = compression fracture; slippage of L5 over S1 = spondylolisthesis; normal in 70% of mechanical LBP |
| **MRI (no contrast)** | Disc morphology, nerve compression, bone marrow oedema, infection, tumour | Red flag (cauda equina, progressive neurology, suspicion of infection/tumour), or radiculopathy not improving after 4–6 weeks | Disc bulge not compressing root = incidental; high signal in vertebral body on STIR = possible infection/ tumour |
| **CT** | Bony detail, pars defect, fracture | Suspected spondylolysis or complex fracture, MRI contraindicated | Pars defect (spondylolysis) or fracture line |
| **Bloods (FBC, CRP, ESR)** | Signs of infection or inflammation | Red flags: fever, IVDU, night pain | WCC/CRP elevated → infection/inflammatory; ESR >100 could be myeloma or sepsis |
| **Urinalysis** | Rule out UTI; protein/Bence-Jones | Back pain + fever in elderly, or suspected multiple myeloma | Proteinuria + back pain → consider myeloma |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **Confirms benign mechanical:** No red flags + normal exam + spontaneous improvement in 2 weeks.
- **Rules out mechanical** (i.e., identifies organic cause): **Any red flag finding** – especially saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, or urine retention (cauda equina). Fever + raised CRP + back pain = infection until proven otherwise.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Reassure:** “Most back pain gets better in a few weeks – you don’t need a scan yet.”
- **Analgesia:**
  - **NSAID** first-line if no contraindication: Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food, for up to 7–10 days. Mechanism: reduces prostaglandin-mediated inflammation at the torn muscle/disc. Alternative: naproxen 500mg PO BD.
  - **Paracetamol** 1g PO QDS (consider weaker evidence, but safe adjunct).
- **Avoid:** Muscle relaxants (diazepam) – weak evidence, high sedation, falls risk. Avoid opioids (evidence of long-term harm).
- **Advice:** Stay active within pain limits. Avoid bed rest >24h. Apply heat packs for muscle spasm.

**Ward / 24–72 hours (if admitted for red flag workup – but mechanical LBP rarely requires admission):**
- If admitted for observation (e.g., unilateral severe pain, cannot mobilise), ensure deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis (mechanical + LMWH if risk factors) because immobility worsens outcome.
- **Physical therapy consult:** Early advice on core strengthening, ergonomics. Do not order traction or lumbar supports – they don’t help.
- **Reassess for red flags** every shift. Escalate if new neurology or fever appears.

**Long-term (chronic / recurrent mechanical LBP):**
- **Exercise therapy:** Individualised programme – McKenzie method (centralisation exercises), core stability, walking. Evidence: reduces recurrence from 40% to 20% (Lancet, 2018).
- **Manual therapy:** Spinal manipulation (osteopath/chiropractor) – modest short-term benefit, no harm.
- **Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT):** For patients with medium/high STarT Back risk. Addresses fear-avoidance behaviours that promote chronicity.
- **Surgery:** Only for radiculopathy (discectomy) or spinal stenosis with claudication – not for mechanical LBP alone. Refer to spinal surgeon if concordant + structural abnormality on MRI.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe **bed rest** >24h – worsens pain and recovery (strong evidence).
- Do not order **imaging** in first 4 weeks unless red flags present.
- Do not start **opioids** for acute LBP (CDC guidelines – risk of long-term use outweighs benefit for mechanical pain).
- Do not inject **facet joints or trigger points** unless diagnostic block confirms source (low-level evidence).

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Mechanical LBP | Radiculopathy (e.g., L5-S1 disc herniation) | Cauda Equina Syndrome | Vertebral Compression Fracture | Metastatic Bone Disease |
|---------|----------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Localised, worse with movement, better rest | Radiating leg pain +/- numbness (dermatomal) | Severe bilateral leg pain / numbness, saddle area | Sharp, focal back pain with axial load (e.g., standing) | Dull, constant ache, worse at night, non-mechanical |
| **Exam** | Paravertebral spasm, limited ROM, no neuro deficit | Positive SLR <60°, weakness, reflex loss | Saddle anaesthesia, loss of anal tone, urinary retention | Localised spinous tenderness, no neuro deficit unless retropulsion | Possible spinal tenderness, may have neuro deficit if epidural spread |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal (unless hypercalcaemia-related arrhythmia) |
| **Labs** | Normal WCC/CRP | Normal WCC/CRP | Normal initially; later may have urinary infection | Normal; if osteoporotic, may have low vitamin D | May have anaemia, raised ESR/CRP, hypercalcaemia, or monoclonal band |
| **Key differentiator** | No red flags; pain improves with activity modification | +SLR <60°, +dermatomal sensory loss, +reflex change | Saddle anaesthesia or urinary retention = emergency | History of fall/ steroids/ osteoporosis; X-ray shows wedge fracture | Night pain, weight loss, age >50, previous cancer |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Chronic low back pain (failure of acute management):**
- How it presents: Pain lasting >12 weeks despite initial treatment. Patient develops fear-avoidance, kinesiophobia, deconditioning.
- Key signs: Negative straight leg raise, widespread tenderness, psychological distress (depression, catastrophizing).
- What to do: Refer to multidisciplinary pain clinic. Consider CBT, graded exercise program. Avoid excessive imaging or injections.

**2. Missed cauda equina syndrome:**
- How it presents: Initially mechanical back pain; then bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence.
- Key signs: Loss of anal tone, decreased perianal sensation, post-void residual >200mL.
- What to do: **Emergency MRI + neurosurgical referral within 24 hours.** Every hour delay to decompression worsens long-term bladder/bowel/erectile dysfunction. This is the most feared consequence of labelling back pain as “mechanical” without a thorough sacral exam.

**3. Missed spinal infection (discitis/osteomyelitis):**
- How it presents: Gradual worsening pain that is constant, worse at night, with fever and rigors in the septic patient. Onset can be insidious over weeks.
- Key signs: Fever (may be absent in elderly), severe spinous percussion tenderness, raised CRP/ESR.
- What to do: Blood cultures + MRI with contrast + empiric antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone) pending cultures. Treat 6–12 weeks IV antibiotics; surgical debridement if abscess >1cm or neurological deficit.

**4. Missed malignant infiltration (e.g., multiple myeloma, metastases):**
- How it presents: Progressive, unremitting back pain in a patient >50 with weight loss. Pain is worse at night (patient awakens from sleep).
- Key signs: Tenderness over multiple spinous processes, constitutional symptoms, elevated ESR or paraprotein.
- What to do: Urgent MRI whole spine + oncology referral. If spinal cord compression: high-dose dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, MRI, and decompression/radiotherapy.

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**
- “70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, severe back pain for 3 weeks, unable to sleep, now nil per os and vomiting.” Think forgotten epidural abscess → sepsis + cord compression → failure to escalate leads to paraplegia.
- “28-year-old woman with postnatal back pain, now leg weakness and numbness ‘down the back of both legs’.” She had a missed cauda equina from herniated disc – now non-recoverable urinary incontinence.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **RED FLAGS** for sinister causes of low back pain (do not call it “mechanical” without checking these):
   T – **T**rauma / **T**umour history  
   A – **A**ge >50 (new onset)  
   R – **R**e- examination for neurological deficits  
   O – **O**steoporosis / steroid use  
   L – **L**oss of bladder/bowel control  
   D – **D**iabetes / immunosuppression (infection)  
   N – **N**ight pain / non-mechanical pattern  
   E – **E**levated ESR/CRP  
   S – **S**ystemic symptoms (fever, weight loss)

2. **SMAIL** for mechanical vs inflammatory back pain (ankylosing spondylitis):
   **S**tiffness: >30 min morning stiffness (inflammatory) vs <5 min (mechanical)  
   **M**ovement: worsens mechanical, improves inflammatory  
   **A**ge: <40 (inflammatory), any age (mechanical)  
   **I**nsidious vs acute onset  
   **L**ocation: buttock/thigh (inflammatory), localised (mechanical)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
- **The most valuable test for mechanical LBP is the history and a neurological exam of sacral function** – not an MRI. The red flags are rare but catastrophic; screen them every time.
- **If a patient can lie comfortably flat, it’s not cauda equina.** Patients with CES cannot lie still due to pain, and often adopt a “sitting forward” posture.
- **Straight leg raise (SLR) reproduces back pain – that’s a negative test (mechanical) unless it reproduces radiating leg pain below the knee.** The former is “hamstring tightness”; the latter is radiculopathy.
- **In acute mechanical LBP, the first 72 hours of NSAIDs are the most effective for pain relief.** After that, inflammation wanes and need declines.
- **Don’t forget the sacroiliac joint** – it can mimic mechanical LBP. Test with FABER (flexion, abduction, external rotation) – if this reproduces the patient’s pain, it’s SIJ, not lumbar.
- **Patients with chronic mechanical LBP and no response to multiple interventions – think about depression, sleep disorders, and opioid-induced hyperalgesia.** The worst trap is ordering more imaging or more injections.
- **“A normal MRI does not mean a normal back.”** Many patients with disabling mechanical LBP have a normal scan – the pain is real, just not structural.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to undress the patient to inspect for cutaneous signs of neurofibromatosis or sacral dimples.
- Assuming bilateral leg pain = sciatica – can be cauda equina. Check saddle.
- Giving an opioid without a bowel regimen – constipation worsens back pain.
- Telling the patient “it’s just a strain” without a full neurological exam – that statement gets you sued when the cauda equina declares itself.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Trigger | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **NEWS2 ≥5** (any red parameter) | Immediate senior review + A-E assessment | Resp rate >24 or <8, SpO2 <91%, HR >130, BP <90/60, GCS <15 |
| **Suspected cauda equina syndrome** | Emergency MRI + neurosurg consult – this is a “call senior immediately” | New bladder/bowel dysfunction + saddle anaesthesia + bilateral leg weakness |
| **Suspected spinal infection with sepsis** | Start IV antibiotics after cultures + senior (+/- ICU if septic shock) | Lactate >2 mmol/L, MAP <65, temp >38.5°C |
| **Acute motor deficit** (e.g., foot drop, quadriceps weakness) | Urgent MRI + spine team referral within 12h (possible herniation) | MRC grade ≤3 in any myotome |
| **NEWS2 triggers for non-specific back pain** (e.g., sudden severe pain with haemodynamic instability) | Consider ruptured AAA mimicking back pain – immediate vascular surgical consult | Shock + abdominal/back pain + pulsatile mass, NEWS ≥6 |
| **GCS drop / respiratory failure** secondary to opioid overdose from prescribed analgesia | Naloxone 0.4mg IV stat, call crash team | Resp rate <10, pinpoint pupils, GCS <8 |

**Specific escalation pathway for “mechanical” label gone wrong:**
If a patient admitted for “mechanical LBP” develops **fever >38.3°C + back tenderness + tachycardia** – immediately stop calling it mechanical. That’s a surgical emergency (epidural abscess). Draw blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics, organise urgent MRI, and call the spinal team.

**Remember:** The junior doctor who misses cauda equina once often carries that scar for their entire career. Escalate early. It is never “just back pain” until you have proven otherwise.

rawMarkdown:
## Low Back Pain — Mechanical
**One-liner:** Benign musculoskeletal pain from lumbar strain or degeneration; affects up to 80% of adults, usually self-limiting, but mimics can kill or paralyze.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 35-year-old manual worker presents to the emergency department after lifting a heavy box at work. He felt a sudden “pop” in his lower back followed by sharp, localised pain. He can only walk if hunched forward, and bending makes it worse. Lying flat with a pillow under his knees eases the pain. He has no leg pain, no numbness, no bladder or bowel disturbance. Vital signs are normal. Straight leg raise is slightly limited by back pain (not by radicular pain) and there is no weakness, reflex loss, or saddle anaesthesia.

**Pattern clues dissected:**
| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Sudden onset with lifting | Acute myofascial strain or disc stress; no time for insidious pathology |
| Pain localised to lower back (no leg pain) | No nerve root irritation; likely muscle, ligament, or facet joint |
| Pain worse with bending, better lying flat | Mechanical nature – movement aggravates, rest relieves (vs inflammatory pain where rest worsens) |
| No leg pain, no neurological signs | Radiculopathy unlikely; cauda equina syndrome ruled out |
| Normal vital signs, no fever | Infection, inflammatory disease, and malignancy less likely |
| Age 35 | Degenerative change unlikely (though possible); discogenic or muscular cause |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Older patient (65+)** with vague, dull low back ache – may be degenerative facet arthritis or undiagnosed compression fracture; “mechanical” label can mask osteoporosis or metastasis.
- **Young athlete** with unilateral lower back pain worse on hyperextension – think spondylolysis (stress fracture of pars interarticularis) rather than simple strain.
- **Postpartum woman** with deep, aching sacral pain – may be sacroiliac joint dysfunction, but also consider occult osteomyelitis pubis or sacral stress fracture.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
Mechanical low back pain is pain originating from the musculoskeletal structures of the lumbar spine (muscles, ligaments, intervertebral discs, facet joints) in the absence of systemic disease, infection, cancer, or nerve compression. It is a diagnosis of exclusion that relies on a benign history and examination.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** ~80% of adults experience at least one episode. It’s the leading cause of years lived with disability globally.
- **Stakes:** Most resolves in 4–6 weeks with simple analgesia and active management. But missing a red flag (cauda equina, infection, fracture, malignancy) can lead to permanent paralysis, sepsis, or death.
- **Common pitfall:** Over-investigation (MRI in the first 4–6 weeks) leads to incidental findings (disc bulges, mild degeneration) that don’t correlate with symptoms, causing unnecessary anxiety and surgery. Conversely, under-investigation of red flags is catastrophic.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how it develops:**
1. **Acute strain:** A sudden force or awkward twist exceeds the microtensile strength of lumbar muscles and ligaments. Small tears → local haemorrhage → inflammatory cascade (prostaglandins, bradykinin) → muscle spasm as a protective reflex. The spasm itself is painful and stiffens the spine.
2. **Degenerative disc / facet joint:** Over years, the annulus fibrosus weakens, allowing the nucleus pulposus to bulge (but not herniate into the canal). Inflammatory mediators from annular tears irritate the sinuvertebral nerve, generating localised pain without root compression. Facet joint osteoarthritis causes chondrocyte injury, joint effusion, and capsular stretch → referred pain to the buttock or posterior thigh.

**Why each key sign happens:**
- **Pain with flexion (bending forward):** Stretches already inflamed posterior ligaments and increases intradiscal pressure; also compresses the anterior annulus.
- **Pain relief lying flat:** Unloads the spine – disc pressure drops from ~100 kg standing to ~25 kg supine.
- **Hunched (antalgic) gait:** Reflex paravertebral muscle spasm splints the spine in a position that maximises canal diameter (slight flexion) and minimises tension on injured structures.
- **Absence of leg pain / neurologic signs:** Because the pathology is confined to the outer disc annulus, muscles, or facet joints – no nerve root compression or cauda equina irritation.

**Connection to pattern recognition:**
The “mechanical” nature (worse with activity, better with rest) differentiates it from **inflammatory** back pain (ankylosing spondylitis) where rest makes it worse and morning stiffness lasts >30 minutes, and from **tumour/infection** pain which is constant, progressive, and often nocturnal.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | 1-line “stem clue” that points to it |
|----------|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | Non-specific back strain | No specific trauma, no red flags, well young adult |
| **Mechanical – acute** | Lumbar muscle/ligament sprain | “Lifting heavy object”, sudden onset, localised tenderness |
| **Mechanical – discogenic** | Degenerative disc disease without herniation | Chronic ache with flares; worse sitting long periods; no leg pain |
| **Mechanical – articular** | Facet joint osteoarthritis | Pain with extension (arching back) and rotation; tenderness paraspinal |
| **Mechanical – structural** | Spondylolysis / spondylolisthesis | Young athlete, pain on hyperextension, may have mild radicular symptoms |
| **Sacroiliac joint dysfunction** | Ligamentous strain of SIJ | Unilateral buttock pain, worse with single-leg stance, positive Fortin finger test |
| **Ligamentous** | Interspinous ligament sprain (e.g., after hyperflexion) | Pain directly over spinous processes; reproduces with springing test |

**Highlight most common cause:** Non-specific mechanical LBP (~90% of all acute episodes). Specific diagnosis often elusive – it’s the pattern that matters, not the label.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
- **STarT Back Screening Tool** (for risk stratification in primary care): 9 questions plus disability scale. Categorises patients into low/medium/high risk. Low risk → reassure, self-manage. Medium/high risk → consider physiotherapy or psychosocial intervention.  
- **No validated diagnostic criteria for mechanical LBP itself** – it’s a clinical diagnosis.

**For each test:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **None (initial)** | – | Red flags absent; most LBP needs no imaging | A recent trial (Lancet, 2023) showed early MRI in simple LBP increased surgery rate without better outcomes |
| **X-ray (2 views)** | Alignment, bone density, disc height, fractures, spondylolisthesis | Suspected fracture (fall/osteoporosis), suspected spondylolisthesis, age >50 with first episode | Loss of height + wedge deformity = compression fracture; slippage of L5 over S1 = spondylolisthesis; normal in 70% of mechanical LBP |
| **MRI (no contrast)** | Disc morphology, nerve compression, bone marrow oedema, infection, tumour | Red flag (cauda equina, progressive neurology, suspicion of infection/tumour), or radiculopathy not improving after 4–6 weeks | Disc bulge not compressing root = incidental; high signal in vertebral body on STIR = possible infection/ tumour |
| **CT** | Bony detail, pars defect, fracture | Suspected spondylolysis or complex fracture, MRI contraindicated | Pars defect (spondylolysis) or fracture line |
| **Bloods (FBC, CRP, ESR)** | Signs of infection or inflammation | Red flags: fever, IVDU, night pain | WCC/CRP elevated → infection/inflammatory; ESR >100 could be myeloma or sepsis |
| **Urinalysis** | Rule out UTI; protein/Bence-Jones | Back pain + fever in elderly, or suspected multiple myeloma | Proteinuria + back pain → consider myeloma |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **Confirms benign mechanical:** No red flags + normal exam + spontaneous improvement in 2 weeks.
- **Rules out mechanical** (i.e., identifies organic cause): **Any red flag finding** – especially saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, or urine retention (cauda equina). Fever + raised CRP + back pain = infection until proven otherwise.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Reassure:** “Most back pain gets better in a few weeks – you don’t need a scan yet.”
- **Analgesia:**
  - **NSAID** first-line if no contraindication: Ibuprofen 400mg PO TDS with food, for up to 7–10 days. Mechanism: reduces prostaglandin-mediated inflammation at the torn muscle/disc. Alternative: naproxen 500mg PO BD.
  - **Paracetamol** 1g PO QDS (consider weaker evidence, but safe adjunct).
- **Avoid:** Muscle relaxants (diazepam) – weak evidence, high sedation, falls risk. Avoid opioids (evidence of long-term harm).
- **Advice:** Stay active within pain limits. Avoid bed rest >24h. Apply heat packs for muscle spasm.

**Ward / 24–72 hours (if admitted for red flag workup – but mechanical LBP rarely requires admission):**
- If admitted for observation (e.g., unilateral severe pain, cannot mobilise), ensure deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis (mechanical + LMWH if risk factors) because immobility worsens outcome.
- **Physical therapy consult:** Early advice on core strengthening, ergonomics. Do not order traction or lumbar supports – they don’t help.
- **Reassess for red flags** every shift. Escalate if new neurology or fever appears.

**Long-term (chronic / recurrent mechanical LBP):**
- **Exercise therapy:** Individualised programme – McKenzie method (centralisation exercises), core stability, walking. Evidence: reduces recurrence from 40% to 20% (Lancet, 2018).
- **Manual therapy:** Spinal manipulation (osteopath/chiropractor) – modest short-term benefit, no harm.
- **Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT):** For patients with medium/high STarT Back risk. Addresses fear-avoidance behaviours that promote chronicity.
- **Surgery:** Only for radiculopathy (discectomy) or spinal stenosis with claudication – not for mechanical LBP alone. Refer to spinal surgeon if concordant + structural abnormality on MRI.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not prescribe **bed rest** >24h – worsens pain and recovery (strong evidence).
- Do not order **imaging** in first 4 weeks unless red flags present.
- Do not start **opioids** for acute LBP (CDC guidelines – risk of long-term use outweighs benefit for mechanical pain).
- Do not inject **facet joints or trigger points** unless diagnostic block confirms source (low-level evidence).

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Mechanical LBP | Radiculopathy (e.g., L5-S1 disc herniation) | Cauda Equina Syndrome | Vertebral Compression Fracture | Metastatic Bone Disease |
|---------|----------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Localised, worse with movement, better rest | Radiating leg pain +/- numbness (dermatomal) | Severe bilateral leg pain / numbness, saddle area | Sharp, focal back pain with axial load (e.g., standing) | Dull, constant ache, worse at night, non-mechanical |
| **Exam** | Paravertebral spasm, limited ROM, no neuro deficit | Positive SLR <60°, weakness, reflex loss | Saddle anaesthesia, loss of anal tone, urinary retention | Localised spinous tenderness, no neuro deficit unless retropulsion | Possible spinal tenderness, may have neuro deficit if epidural spread |
| **ECG** | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal (unless hypercalcaemia-related arrhythmia) |
| **Labs** | Normal WCC/CRP | Normal WCC/CRP | Normal initially; later may have urinary infection | Normal; if osteoporotic, may have low vitamin D | May have anaemia, raised ESR/CRP, hypercalcaemia, or monoclonal band |
| **Key differentiator** | No red flags; pain improves with activity modification | +SLR <60°, +dermatomal sensory loss, +reflex change | Saddle anaesthesia or urinary retention = emergency | History of fall/ steroids/ osteoporosis; X-ray shows wedge fracture | Night pain, weight loss, age >50, previous cancer |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Chronic low back pain (failure of acute management):**
- How it presents: Pain lasting >12 weeks despite initial treatment. Patient develops fear-avoidance, kinesiophobia, deconditioning.
- Key signs: Negative straight leg raise, widespread tenderness, psychological distress (depression, catastrophizing).
- What to do: Refer to multidisciplinary pain clinic. Consider CBT, graded exercise program. Avoid excessive imaging or injections.

**2. Missed cauda equina syndrome:**
- How it presents: Initially mechanical back pain; then bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence.
- Key signs: Loss of anal tone, decreased perianal sensation, post-void residual >200mL.
- What to do: **Emergency MRI + neurosurgical referral within 24 hours.** Every hour delay to decompression worsens long-term bladder/bowel/erectile dysfunction. This is the most feared consequence of labelling back pain as “mechanical” without a thorough sacral exam.

**3. Missed spinal infection (discitis/osteomyelitis):**
- How it presents: Gradual worsening pain that is constant, worse at night, with fever and rigors in the septic patient. Onset can be insidious over weeks.
- Key signs: Fever (may be absent in elderly), severe spinous percussion tenderness, raised CRP/ESR.
- What to do: Blood cultures + MRI with contrast + empiric antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone) pending cultures. Treat 6–12 weeks IV antibiotics; surgical debridement if abscess >1cm or neurological deficit.

**4. Missed malignant infiltration (e.g., multiple myeloma, metastases):**
- How it presents: Progressive, unremitting back pain in a patient >50 with weight loss. Pain is worse at night (patient awakens from sleep).
- Key signs: Tenderness over multiple spinous processes, constitutional symptoms, elevated ESR or paraprotein.
- What to do: Urgent MRI whole spine + oncology referral. If spinal cord compression: high-dose dexamethasone 16mg IV stat, MRI, and decompression/radiotherapy.

**Late-presentation patterns (the stems that test “what happens if untreated”):**
- “70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, severe back pain for 3 weeks, unable to sleep, now nil per os and vomiting.” Think forgotten epidural abscess → sepsis + cord compression → failure to escalate leads to paraplegia.
- “28-year-old woman with postnatal back pain, now leg weakness and numbness ‘down the back of both legs’.” She had a missed cauda equina from herniated disc – now non-recoverable urinary incontinence.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **RED FLAGS** for sinister causes of low back pain (do not call it “mechanical” without checking these):
   T – **T**rauma / **T**umour history  
   A – **A**ge >50 (new onset)  
   R – **R**e- examination for neurological deficits  
   O – **O**steoporosis / steroid use  
   L – **L**oss of bladder/bowel control  
   D – **D**iabetes / immunosuppression (infection)  
   N – **N**ight pain / non-mechanical pattern  
   E – **E**levated ESR/CRP  
   S – **S**ystemic symptoms (fever, weight loss)

2. **SMAIL** for mechanical vs inflammatory back pain (ankylosing spondylitis):
   **S**tiffness: >30 min morning stiffness (inflammatory) vs <5 min (mechanical)  
   **M**ovement: worsens mechanical, improves inflammatory  
   **A**ge: <40 (inflammatory), any age (mechanical)  
   **I**nsidious vs acute onset  
   **L**ocation: buttock/thigh (inflammatory), localised (mechanical)

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
- **The most valuable test for mechanical LBP is the history and a neurological exam of sacral function** – not an MRI. The red flags are rare but catastrophic; screen them every time.
- **If a patient can lie comfortably flat, it’s not cauda equina.** Patients with CES cannot lie still due to pain, and often adopt a “sitting forward” posture.
- **Straight leg raise (SLR) reproduces back pain – that’s a negative test (mechanical) unless it reproduces radiating leg pain below the knee.** The former is “hamstring tightness”; the latter is radiculopathy.
- **In acute mechanical LBP, the first 72 hours of NSAIDs are the most effective for pain relief.** After that, inflammation wanes and need declines.
- **Don’t forget the sacroiliac joint** – it can mimic mechanical LBP. Test with FABER (flexion, abduction, external rotation) – if this reproduces the patient’s pain, it’s SIJ, not lumbar.
- **Patients with chronic mechanical LBP and no response to multiple interventions – think about depression, sleep disorders, and opioid-induced hyperalgesia.** The worst trap is ordering more imaging or more injections.
- **“A normal MRI does not mean a normal back.”** Many patients with disabling mechanical LBP have a normal scan – the pain is real, just not structural.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to undress the patient to inspect for cutaneous signs of neurofibromatosis or sacral dimples.
- Assuming bilateral leg pain = sciatica – can be cauda equina. Check saddle.
- Giving an opioid without a bowel regimen – constipation worsens back pain.
- Telling the patient “it’s just a strain” without a full neurological exam – that statement gets you sued when the cauda equina declares itself.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Trigger | Action | Threshold |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **NEWS2 ≥5** (any red parameter) | Immediate senior review + A-E assessment | Resp rate >24 or <8, SpO2 <91%, HR >130, BP <90/60, GCS <15 |
| **Suspected cauda equina syndrome** | Emergency MRI + neurosurg consult – this is a “call senior immediately” | New bladder/bowel dysfunction + saddle anaesthesia + bilateral leg weakness |
| **Suspected spinal infection with sepsis** | Start IV antibiotics after cultures + senior (+/- ICU if septic shock) | Lactate >2 mmol/L, MAP <65, temp >38.5°C |
| **Acute motor deficit** (e.g., foot drop, quadriceps weakness) | Urgent MRI + spine team referral within 12h (possible herniation) | MRC grade ≤3 in any myotome |
| **NEWS2 triggers for non-specific back pain** (e.g., sudden severe pain with haemodynamic instability) | Consider ruptured AAA mimicking back pain – immediate vascular surgical consult | Shock + abdominal/back pain + pulsatile mass, NEWS ≥6 |
| **GCS drop / respiratory failure** secondary to opioid overdose from prescribed analgesia | Naloxone 0.4mg IV stat, call crash team | Resp rate <10, pinpoint pupils, GCS <8 |

**Specific escalation pathway for “mechanical” label gone wrong:**
If a patient admitted for “mechanical LBP” develops **fever >38.3°C + back tenderness + tachycardia** – immediately stop calling it mechanical. That’s a surgical emergency (epidural abscess). Draw blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics, organise urgent MRI, and call the spinal team.

**Remember:** The junior doctor who misses cauda equina once often carries that scar for their entire career. Escalate early. It is never “just back pain” until you have proven otherwise.
